Implement the following plan:

# Replace xterm.js with ghostty-web + Single Terminal Per Branch

## Context
The embedded xterm.js terminal is low quality. Replace it with ghostty-web (Ghostty's VT parser compiled to WASM, xterm.js-compatible API). Also simplify terminal management: one session per branch instead of multiple sessions, remove the "+" new session button. The "Send to Agent" from the review bar should launch a new Ghostty external window with the prompt.

## Changes

### 1. Swap xterm.js → ghostty-web in `src/features/terminal/XTerminal.tsx`
- Replace `@xterm/xterm` import with `import { init, Terminal } from 'ghostty-web'`
- Remove `@xterm/xterm/css/xterm.css` import (ghostty-web has no CSS dependency)
- Remove `@xterm/addon-fit` — use manual resize via `term.resize(cols, rows)` with a helper that calculates cols/rows from container dimensions (character width/height measurement)
- Add one-time `await init()` call before creating Terminal (loads WASM module)
- Keep the same PTY communication pattern (`pty-output` event → `term.write()`, `term.onData()` → `pty_input`)
- Keep the same theme/font config (ghostty-web accepts same options)
- Update welcome message: remove "Click 'New Session'" text, replace with "Launch an agent to get started"

### 2. Simplify `src/features/terminal/TerminalPanel.tsx` — one session per branch
**Remove:**
- Multi-session `sessions[]` array and `activeSessionIdx`
- Session tab buttons (the row of clickable session tabs)
- The "+" button that cycles agents

**Replace with:**
- Single `session: TermSession | null` state
- Header shows: agent label (if running) + agent picker dropdown + Launch/Kill buttons + toggle
- When no session: show agent picker (Claude Code / Codex) + "Launch" button
- When session exists: show status indicator + agent label + "Kill" button
- Keep `forwardRef` + `useImperativeHandle` for `launchAgentWithPrompt` (used by review bar)

**Layout:**
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ● Claude Code — main              [Kill]   ⌃` ▾    │  ← header
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                     │
│  ghostty-web terminal                               │  ← terminal body
│                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

When no session:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  [Claude Code ▾]  [Launch ▸]               ⌃` ▾    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                     │
│  Welcome / empty state                              │
│                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 3. Attach terminal to branch in `src/App.tsx`
- Lift session state from TerminalPanel up to App.tsx
- Store sessions in a `Map<tabId, TermSession>` so each branch tab has its own session
- When switching tabs, pass the correct session to TerminalPanel
- TerminalPanel becomes a controlled component: receives `session` as a prop instead of managing its own
- The PTY session persists when switching between tabs (XTerminal reconnects to the existing sessionId)

### 4. Update "Send to Agent" from ReviewBar
- Currently calls `terminalRef.current.launchAgentWithPrompt()` which spawns a PTY session in the embedded terminal
- Change: Launch a new **external Ghostty window** with the agent and prompt
- New Rust command: `launch_ghostty_agent(repo_path: String, agent: String, prompt: String) -> Result<(), String>`
  - Writes prompt to a temp file
  - Spawns: `ghostty -e "cd <repo_path> && <agent> -p <temp_file_path>"`
  - Fire-and-forget (no session tracking needed for external windows)
- Update `App.tsx` `handleSendToAgent` to call the new Rust command instead of the terminal ref
- The embedded terminal remains for interactive shell/agent use; review dispatch opens a separate Ghostty window

### 5. npm dependency changes
```bash
npm install ghostty-web
npm uninstall @xterm/xterm @xterm/addon-fit
```

## Files to Modify
1. `src/features/terminal/XTerminal.tsx` — swap xterm.js → ghostty-web, add `init()`, remove FitAddon
2. `src/features/terminal/TerminalPanel.tsx` — single session, simplified header, controlled component
3. `src/App.tsx` — lift session map, wire per-branch sessions, update handleSendToAgent
4. `src/features/diff/ReviewBar.tsx` — update "Send to Agent" to use new external Ghostty command
5. `src-tauri/src/pty/mod.rs` — add `launch_ghostty_agent` function
6. `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` — register new Tauri command
7. `package.json` — dependency swap

## Files Unchanged
- `src/hooks/use-diff-comments.ts` — no changes
- `src/features/diff/DiffCommentBox.tsx` — no changes
- `src/features/diff/build-review-prompt.ts` — no changes
- `src/features/diff/DiffView.tsx` — no changes (still passes `onSendToAgent` down)

## Verification
1. `npm install` succeeds with ghostty-web
2. `npx tsc --noEmit` — zero type errors
3. `cargo check` in src-tauri — zero errors
4. Open app → terminal panel shows at bottom with ghostty-web rendering
5. Launch Claude Code → session starts, terminal is interactive
6. Switch to another branch tab → terminal shows that branch's session (or empty state)
7. Switch back → previous session still running and visible
8. Kill session → returns to empty state with launch controls
9. Add diff comments → click "Send to Agent" → external Ghostty window opens with agent + prompt
10. No "+" button to create additional sessions


If you need specific details from before exiting plan mode (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/alisha/.claude/projects/-Users-alisha-Projects-entire-app/a89882d2-a54c-414e-a40b-2796f61ac599.jsonl

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**ghostty-web** is Ghostty's VT parser compiled to WebAssembly. It provides an xterm.js-compatible API but with better rendering quality. The key difference is requiring an async `init()` call to load the WASM module before creating terminals. This is a common pattern for WASM-based libraries in web apps.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Let me start by reading the existing files to understand the current implementation.

content.text:
Both TypeScript and Rust compile cleanly with zero errors.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**Lifted state pattern**: The terminal session state was moved from `TerminalPanel` (local) to `App.tsx` (parent) using a `Map<tabId, TermSession>`. This is a common React pattern called "lifting state up" — it enables the terminal to persist sessions across tab switches. The `TerminalPanel` becomes a **controlled component** receiving `session` as a prop rather than managing its own state.

**WASM initialization singleton**: The `ensureInit()` pattern in `XTerminal.tsx` ensures the WASM module loads exactly once, even if multiple terminal instances mount concurrently. This avoids redundant WASM compilation and potential race conditions.

**Graceful fallback**: The `handleSendToAgent` in `App.tsx` attempts the external Ghostty window first, but falls back to the embedded terminal if Ghostty isn't installed — this ensures the feature degrades gracefully.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Here's a summary of all changes:

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `package.json` | Added `ghostty-web`, removed `@xterm/xterm`, `@xterm/addon-fit`, `@xterm/addon-webgl` |
| `src/features/terminal/XTerminal.tsx` | Swapped imports to `ghostty-web`, added async `init()` via singleton, removed `lineHeight` (not supported), updated welcome message |
| `src/features/terminal/TerminalPanel.tsx` | Single session (controlled via props), removed multi-session tabs/`+` button, added Kill button, exports `TermSession` type |
| `src/App.tsx` | Added `sessionMap` (Map<tabId, TermSession>), passes per-branch session to TerminalPanel, `handleSendToAgent` now calls `launch_ghostty_agent` with embedded terminal fallback, cleans up sessions on tab close |
| `src-tauri/src/pty/mod.rs` | Added `launch_ghostty_agent()` — writes prompt to temp file, spawns `ghostty -e sh -c "cd <repo> && <agent> -p <file>"` |
| `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` | Registered `launch_ghostty_agent` Tauri command |

1→# ghostty-web
     2→
     3→[![NPM Version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/ghostty-web)](https://npmjs.com/package/ghostty-web) [![NPM Downloads](https://img.shields.io/npm/dw/ghostty-web)](https://npmjs.com/package/ghostty-web) [![npm bundle size](https://img.shields.io/bundlephobia/minzip/ghostty-web)](https://npmjs.com/package/ghostty-web) [![license](https://img.shields.io/github/license/coder/ghostty-web)](./LICENSE)
     4→
     5→[Ghostty](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty) for the web with [xterm.js](https://github.com/xtermjs/xterm.js) API compatibility — giving you a proper VT100 implementation in the browser.
     6→
     7→- Migrate from xterm by changing your import: `@xterm/xterm` → `ghostty-web`
     8→- WASM-compiled parser from Ghostty—the same code that runs the native app
     9→- Zero runtime dependencies, ~400KB WASM bundle
    10→
    11→Originally created for [Mux](https://github.com/coder/mux) (a desktop app for isolated, parallel agentic development), but designed to be used anywhere.
    12→
    13→## Try It
    14→
    15→- [Live Demo](https://ghostty.ondis.co) on an ephemeral VM (thank you to Greg from [disco.cloud](https://disco.cloud) for hosting).
    16→
    17→- On your computer:
    18→
    19→  ```bash
    20→  npx @ghostty-web/demo@next
    21→  ```
    22→
    23→  This starts a local HTTP server with a real shell on `http://localhost:8080`. Works best on Linux and macOS.
    24→
    25→![ghostty](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aceee7eb-d57b-4d89-ac3d-ee1885d0187a)
    26→
    27→## Comparison with xterm.js
    28→
    29→xterm.js is everywhere—VS Code, Hyper, countless web terminals. But it has fundamental issues:
    30→
    31→| Issue                                    | xterm.js                                                         | ghostty-web                |
    32→| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
    33→| **Complex scripts** (Devanagari, Arabic) | Rendering issues                                                 | ✓ Proper grapheme handling |
    34→| **XTPUSHSGR/XTPOPSGR**                   | [Not supported](https://github.com/xtermjs/xterm.js/issues/2570) | ✓ Full support             |
    35→
    36→xterm.js reimplements terminal emulation in JavaScript. Every escape sequence, every edge case, every Unicode quirk—all hand-coded. Ghostty's emulator is the same battle-tested code that runs the native Ghostty app.
    37→
    38→## Installation
    39→
    40→```bash
    41→npm install ghostty-web
    42→```
    43→
    44→## Usage
    45→
    46→ghostty-web aims to be API-compatible with the xterm.js API.
    47→
    48→```javascript
    49→import { init, Terminal } from 'ghostty-web';
    50→
    51→await init();
    52→
    53→const term = new Terminal({
    54→  fontSize: 14,
    55→  theme: {
    56→    background: '#1a1b26',
    57→    foreground: '#a9b1d6',
    58→  },
    59→});
    60→
    61→term.open(document.getElementById('terminal'));
    62→term.onData((data) => websocket.send(data));
    63→websocket.onmessage = (e) => term.write(e.data);
    64→```
    65→
    66→For a comprehensive client <-> server example, refer to the [demo](./demo/index.html#L141).
    67→
    68→## Development
    69→
    70→ghostty-web builds from Ghostty's source with a [patch](./patches/ghostty-wasm-api.patch) to expose additional
    71→functionality.
    72→
    73→> Requires Zig and Bun.
    74→
    75→```bash
    76→bun run build
    77→```
    78→
    79→Mitchell Hashimoto (author of Ghostty) has [been working](https://mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming) on `libghostty` which makes this all possible. The patches are very minimal thanks to the work the Ghostty team has done, and we expect them to get smaller.
    80→
    81→This library will eventually consume a native Ghostty WASM distribution once available, and will continue to provide an xterm.js compatible API.
    82→
    83→At Coder we're big fans of Ghostty, so kudos to that team for all the amazing work.
    84→
    85→## License
    86→
    87→[MIT](./LICENSE)
    88→

content.content:
1→# ghostty-web
     2→
     3→[![NPM Version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/ghostty-web)](https://npmjs.com/package/ghostty-web) [![NPM Downloads](https://img.shields.io/npm/dw/ghostty-web)](https://npmjs.com/package/ghostty-web) [![npm bundle size](https://img.shields.io/bundlephobia/minzip/ghostty-web)](https://npmjs.com/package/ghostty-web) [![license](https://img.shields.io/github/license/coder/ghostty-web)](./LICENSE)
     4→
     5→[Ghostty](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty) for the web with [xterm.js](https://github.com/xtermjs/xterm.js) API compatibility — giving you a proper VT100 implementation in the browser.
     6→
     7→- Migrate from xterm by changing your import: `@xterm/xterm` → `ghostty-web`
     8→- WASM-compiled parser from Ghostty—the same code that runs the native app
     9→- Zero runtime dependencies, ~400KB WASM bundle
    10→
    11→Originally created for [Mux](https://github.com/coder/mux) (a desktop app for isolated, parallel agentic development), but designed to be used anywhere.
    12→
    13→## Try It
    14→
    15→- [Live Demo](https://ghostty.ondis.co) on an ephemeral VM (thank you to Greg from [disco.cloud](https://disco.cloud) for hosting).
    16→
    17→- On your computer:
    18→
    19→  ```bash
    20→  npx @ghostty-web/demo@next
    21→  ```
    22→
    23→  This starts a local HTTP server with a real shell on `http://localhost:8080`. Works best on Linux and macOS.
    24→
    25→![ghostty](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aceee7eb-d57b-4d89-ac3d-ee1885d0187a)
    26→
    27→## Comparison with xterm.js
    28→
    29→xterm.js is everywhere—VS Code, Hyper, countless web terminals. But it has fundamental issues:
    30→
    31→| Issue                                    | xterm.js                                                         | ghostty-web                |
    32→| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
    33→| **Complex scripts** (Devanagari, Arabic) | Rendering issues                                                 | ✓ Proper grapheme handling |
    34→| **XTPUSHSGR/XTPOPSGR**                   | [Not supported](https://github.com/xtermjs/xterm.js/issues/2570) | ✓ Full support             |
    35→
    36→xterm.js reimplements terminal emulation in JavaScript. Every escape sequence, every edge case, every Unicode quirk—all hand-coded. Ghostty's emulator is the same battle-tested code that runs the native Ghostty app.
    37→
    38→## Installation
    39→
    40→```bash
    41→npm install ghostty-web
    42→```
    43→
    44→## Usage
    45→
    46→ghostty-web aims to be API-compatible with the xterm.js API.
    47→
    48→```javascript
    49→import { init, Terminal } from 'ghostty-web';
    50→
    51→await init();
    52→
    53→const term = new Terminal({
    54→  fontSize: 14,
    55→  theme: {
    56→    background: '#1a1b26',
    57→    foreground: '#a9b1d6',
    58→  },
    59→});
    60→
    61→term.open(document.getElementById('terminal'));
    62→term.onData((data) => websocket.send(data));
    63→websocket.onmessage = (e) => term.write(e.data);
    64→```
    65→
    66→For a comprehensive client <-> server example, refer to the [demo](./demo/index.html#L141).
    67→
    68→## Development
    69→
    70→ghostty-web builds from Ghostty's source with a [patch](./patches/ghostty-wasm-api.patch) to expose additional
    71→functionality.
    72→
    73→> Requires Zig and Bun.
    74→
    75→```bash
    76→bun run build
    77→```
    78→
    79→Mitchell Hashimoto (author of Ghostty) has [been working](https://mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming) on `libghostty` which makes this all possible. The patches are very minimal thanks to the work the Ghostty team has done, and we expect them to get smaller.
    80→
    81→This library will eventually consume a native Ghostty WASM distribution once available, and will continue to provide an xterm.js compatible API.
    82→
    83→At Coder we're big fans of Ghostty, so kudos to that team for all the amazing work.
    84→
    85→## License
    86→
    87→[MIT](./LICENSE)
    88→

Perfect! Now I have comprehensive research completed. Let me compile all my findings:

## Summary of ghostty-web Configuration Research

I've completed a thorough analysis of the ghostty-web package and how it can be configured. Here are all the findings:

### 1. **README.md Overview**
The ghostty-web library is:
- WASM-compiled parser from Ghostty (same code as native app)
- ~400KB WASM bundle
- xterm.js API compatible
- Uses a single `.wasm` file (`ghostty-vt.wasm`)

### 2. **The `ghostty?: Ghostty` Option in ITerminalOptions**

The `ghostty` option accepts a **pre-loaded Ghostty instance**. This is used for:
- **Advanced scenarios** (primarily testing): You can pre-load a Ghostty WASM instance and pass it to the Terminal constructor
- **Key encoding**: The Ghostty instance contains a `KeyEncoder` that handles keyboard input processing
- **Terminal emulation**: The instance is used internally to create the low-level `GhosttyTerminal` for WASM-based emulation

```typescript
// Standard usage (auto-loads WASM):
await init();
const term = new Terminal({ /* options */ });

// Advanced usage (pass pre-loaded instance):
const ghostty = await Ghostty.load();
const term = new Terminal({ ghostty });
```

### 3. **Customization Available in ghostty-web**

The library provides extensive customization through **ITerminalOptions** and **setter methods**:

**Constructor Options:**
- `cols` / `rows` - Terminal dimensions
- `fontSize` - Font size in pixels
- `fontFamily` - Font family (CSS font stack)
- `cursorBlink` - Enable/disable cursor blinking
- `cursorStyle` - 'block' | 'underline' | 'bar'
- `theme` - Full color theme (ITheme interface)
- `scrollback` - Scrollback buffer lines
- `allowTransparency` - Background transparency
- `convertEol` - Convert EOL characters
- `disableStdin` - Disable keyboard input
- `smoothScrollDuration` - Smooth scroll animation duration

**Runtime Setter Methods:**
- `setTheme(theme: ITheme)` - Update colors dynamically
- `setFontSize(size: number)` - Change font size
- `setFontFamily(family: string)` - Change font family
- `setCursorStyle(style: 'block' | 'underline' | 'bar')` - Update cursor style
- `setCursorBlink(enabled: boolean)` - Toggle cursor blink

**ITheme Interface** (22 color properties):
```typescript
foreground, background, cursor, cursorAccent, selectionBackground,
selectionForeground, black, red, green, yellow, blue, magenta, cyan, white,
brightBlack, brightRed, brightGreen, brightYellow, brightBlue,
brightMagenta, brightCyan, brightWhite
```

### 4. **Native Ghostty Config File Reading**

**CRITICAL FINDING:** ghostty-web **CANNOT parse or read native Ghostty config files**.

Evidence:
- No config file parsing functions in the API
- No file I/O capabilities in the WASM module
- The library only accepts **programmatically-provided configuration** through the Terminal options
- You must manually extract settings from Ghostty and pass them as parameters

The `GhosttyTerminalConfig` (low-level WASM config) only supports:
- `scrollbackLimit` - Number of scrollback lines
- `fgColor` - Foreground color (0xRRGGBB format)
- `bgColor` - Background color (0xRRGGBB format)
- `cursorColor` - Cursor color (0xRRGGBB format)
- `palette` - Color palette array (0xRRGGBB format)

### 5. **User's Current Ghostty Setup**

The user does **NOT** have a native Ghostty config file at `~/.config/ghostty/config`. However, there is:
- `~/Library/Preferences/com.mitchellh.ghostty.plist` - Ghostty macOS preferences (binary format)
- No native Ghostty installation in PATH

### 6. **Current Terminal Configuration in the App**

The app hardcodes this configuration in `/Users/alisha/Projects/entire-app/src/features/terminal/XTerminal.tsx`:

```typescript
fontFamily: "'SF Mono', 'Fira Code', 'Cascadia Code', monospace"
fontSize: 13
cursorBlink: true
cursorStyle: "block"
theme: {
  background: "#0a0a0a" (almost black)
  foreground: "#e4e4e7" (light gray)
  cursor: "#7c3aed" (purple)
  cursorAccent: "#0a0a0a"
  selectionBackground: "#7c3aed44" (purple with alpha)
  // 16 color palette: red, green, yellow, blue, magenta, cyan, white
  // + bright variants
}
```

### 7. **Pathway to Match Native Ghostty Setup**

To sync ghostty-web with a native Ghostty installation, you would need to:

1. **Parse native config file** (if it exists at `~/.config/ghostty/config`)
   - Use a Rust backend to read and parse the config
   - Or use Node.js to read the file before launching the browser
   
2. **Extract key settings** from the config:
   - Font family and size
   - Cursor style and blink
   - Color palette/theme
   - Padding (not supported by ghostty-web currently)
   - Other Ghostty-specific features

3. **Pass to Terminal constructor** via ITerminalOptions

4. **Use setter methods** to update theme/fonts at runtime if needed

**File path to update:** `/Users/alisha/Projects/entire-app/src/features/terminal/XTerminal.tsx`

---

All findings are based on reading the complete TypeScript definitions in `/Users/alisha/Projects/entire-app/node_modules/ghostty-web/dist/index.d.ts` and examining the app's current usage in XTerminal.tsx.

   387→/// Coalesces Ghostty background notifications so consumers only observe
   388→/// the latest runtime background for a burst of updates.
   389→final class GhosttyDefaultBackgroundNotificationDispatcher {
   390→    private let coalescer: NotificationBurstCoalescer
   391→    private let postNotification: ([AnyHashable: Any]) -> Void
   392→    private var pendingUserInfo: [AnyHashable: Any]?
   393→    private var pendingEventId: UInt64 = 0
   394→    private var pendingSource: String = "unspecified"
   395→    private let logEvent: ((String) -> Void)?
   396→
   397→    init(
   398→        delay: TimeInterval = 1.0 / 30.0,
   399→        logEvent: ((String) -> Void)? = nil,
   400→        postNotification: @escaping ([AnyHashable: Any]) -> Void = { userInfo in
   401→            NotificationCenter.default.post(
   402→                name: .ghosttyDefaultBackgroundDidChange,
   403→                object: nil,
   404→                userInfo: userInfo
   405→            )
   406→        }
   407→    ) {
   408→        coalescer = NotificationBurstCoalescer(delay: delay)
   409→        self.logEvent = logEvent
   410→        self.postNotification = postNotification
   411→    }
   412→
   413→    func signal(backgroundColor: NSColor, opacity: Double, eventId: UInt64, source: String) {
   414→        let signalOnMain = { [self] in
   415→            pendingEventId = eventId
   416→            pendingSource = source
   417→            pendingUserInfo = [
   418→                GhosttyNotificationKey.backgroundColor: backgroundColor,
   419→                GhosttyNotificationKey.backgroundOpacity: opacity,
   420→                GhosttyNotificationKey.backgroundEventId: NSNumber(value: eventId),
   421→                GhosttyNotificationKey.backgroundSource: source
   422→            ]
   423→            logEvent?(
   424→                "bg notify queued id=\(eventId) source=\(source) color=\(backgroundColor.hexString()) opacity=\(String(format: "%.3f", opacity))"
   425→            )
   426→            coalescer.signal { [self] in
   427→                guard let userInfo = pendingUserInfo else { return }
   428→                let eventId = pendingEventId
   429→                let source = pendingSource
   430→                pendingUserInfo = nil
   431→                logEvent?("bg notify flushed id=\(eventId) source=\(source)")
   432→                logEvent?("bg notify posting id=\(eventId) source=\(source)")
   433→                postNotification(userInfo)
   434→                logEvent?("bg notify posted id=\(eventId) source=\(source)")
   435→            }
   436→        }
   437→
   438→        if Thread.isMainThread {
   439→            signalOnMain()
   440→        } else {
   441→            DispatchQueue.main.async(execute: signalOnMain)
   442→        }
   443→    }
   444→}

67→ 68→- **Scriptable** — CLI and socket API to create workspaces, split panes, send keystrokes, and automate the browser 69→- **Native macOS app** — Built with Swift and AppKit, not Electron. Fast startup, low memory. 70→- **Ghostty compatible** — Reads your existing `~/.config/ghostty/config` for themes, fonts, and colors 71→- **GPU-accelerated** — Powered by libghostty for smooth rendering 72→ 73→## Install 74→ 75→### DMG (recommended) 76→ 77→ 78→ 79→ 80→ 81→Open the `.dmg` and drag cmux to your Applications folder. cmux auto-updates via Sparkle, so you only need to download once. 82→ 83→### Homebrew 84→ 85→```bash 86→brew tap manaflow-ai/cmux 87→brew install --cask cmux 88→``` 89→ 90→To update later: 91→ 92→```bash 93→brew upgrade --cask cmux 94→``` 95→ 96→On first launch, macOS may ask you to confirm opening an app from an identified developer. Click **Open** to proceed. 97→ 98→## Why cmux? 99→ 100→I run a lot of Claude Code and Codex sessions in parallel. I was using Ghostty with a bunch of split panes, and relying on native macOS notifications to know when an agent needed me. But Claude Code's notification body is always just "Claude is waiting for your input" with no context, and with enough tabs open I couldn't even read the titles anymore. 101→ 102→I tried a few coding orchestrators but most of them were Electron/Tauri apps and the performance bugged me. I also just prefer the terminal since GUI orchestrators lock you into their workflow. So I built cmux as a native macOS app in Swift/AppKit. It uses libghostty for terminal rendering and reads your existing Ghostty config for themes, fonts, and colors. 103→ 104→The main additions are the sidebar and notification system. The sidebar has vertical tabs that show git branch, linked PR status/number, working directory, listening ports, and the latest notification text for each workspace. The notification system picks up terminal sequences (OSC 9/99/777) and has a CLI (`cmux notify`) you can wire into agent hooks for Claude Code, OpenCode, etc. When an agent is waiting, its pane gets a blue ring and the tab lights up in the sidebar, so I can tell which one needs me across splits and tabs. Cmd+Shift+U jumps to the most recent unread. 105→ 106→The in-app browser has a scriptable API ported from [agent-browser](https://github.com/vercel-labs/agent-browser). Agents can snapshot the accessibility tree, get element refs, click, fill forms, and evaluate JS. You can split a browser pane next to your terminal and have Claude Code interact with your dev server directly. 107→ 108→Everything is scriptable through the CLI and socket API — create workspaces/tabs, split panes, send keystrokes, open URLs in the browser. 109→ 110→## The Zen of cmux 111→ 112→cmux is not prescriptive about how developers hold their tools. It's a terminal and browser with a CLI, and the rest is up to you. 113→ 114→cmux is a primitive, not a solution. It gives you a terminal, a browser, notifications, workspaces, splits, tabs, and a CLI to control all of it. cmux doesn't force you into an opinionated way to use coding agents. What you build with the primitives is yours. 115→ 116→The best developers have always built their own tools. Nobody has figured out the best way to work with agents yet, and the teams building closed products definitely haven't either. The developers closest to their own codebases will figure it out first. 117→ 118→Give a million developers composable primitives and they'll collectively find the most efficient workflows faster than any product team could design top-down. 119→ 120→## Documentation 121→ 122→For more info on how to configure cmux, [head over to our docs](https://cmux.dev/docs/getting-started?utm_source=readme).
